en.extract:
The Tacarcuna chlorospingus (Chlorospingus tacarcunae) or Tacarcuna bush tanager is a species of bird in the family Passerellidae, the New World sparrows. It is found in Colombia and Panama.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Tacarcuna chlorospingus has a complicated taxonomic history. It was formally described in 1924 with its current binomial Chlorospingus tacarcunae. During the next two decades different authors treated it as a subspecies what are now the yellow-throated chlorospingus (C. flavigularis) or the common chlorospingus (C. flavopectus). From about 1976 it has been again treated as a full species. During this period the Chlorospingus species were members of the family Thraupidae, the "true" tanagers, though their position within the family were uncertain. Based on studies published in 2002, 2003, and 2007, beginning in 2010 taxonomic systems transferred Chlorospingus to its present sparrow family. The Chlorospingus species were originally called "bush tanagers" or "bush-tanagers". Beginning in 2013 systems began changing the English name of the members of the genus to "chlorospingus" because they were no longer considered tanagers. 
The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is monotypic.


Description
The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is 13 to 14 cm (5.1 to 5.5 in) long and weighs about 18 g (0.63 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a mostly dark olive head with a pale yellow throat. Their upperparts are dark olive. Their flight feathers are dusky with greenish yellow edges on the primaries, olive edges on the secondaries, and dark olive green edges on the tertials. Their tail is dusky with an olive tinge and olive green feather edges. Their upper breast is dull yellow, their sides, flanks, and undertail coverts greenish, and the middle of their breast and their belly dull yellowish. Both sexes have a pale orange iris, a black bill, and brownish gray legs and feet. 


Distribution and habitat
The Tacarcuna chlorospingus has a highly disjunct distribution. It is found on individual or abutting mountains from eastern Panamá and Colón provinces in central Panama to Darién Province and on both sides of Cerro Tacarcuna that straddles the Panama-Colombia border. It inhabits the interior and edges of montane evergreen forest and also elfin forest in the subtropical and lower temperate zones. In elevation it ranges overall between 850 and 1,500 m (2,800 and 4,900 ft). In Panamá Province it ranges between 850 and 1,000 m (2,800 and 3,300 ft) and on the Panamanian side of Cerro Tacarcuna between 1,230 and 1,440 m (4,000 and 4,700 ft). On the Colombian side it ranges between 1,200 and 1,500 m (3,900 and 4,900 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is a year-round resident.


Feeding
The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is known to feed on insects and is assumed to feed on fruits as well like others of its genus. It forages at all levels of the forest. It forages in pairs and groups of up to about six individuals and regularly joins mixed-species feeding flocks.


Breeding
The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is thought to breed between February and August. The one known nest was in a bromeliad clump on a branch about 6.5 m (20 ft) above the ground. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.


Vocalization
As of April 2026 xeno-canto had three recordings of Tacarcuna chlorospingus calls; the Cornell Lab's Macaulay Library had 15 others. Neither archive had recordings of its song. The species' call is described as "a single high-pitched tseeeu!".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Tacarcuna chlorospingus as being of Least Concern. It has a restricted range and its estimated population of at least 20,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. The only expected threat is stress on its habitat due to climate change. "[The] Tacarcuna Chlorospingus has been always recorded in pristine or well conserved habitats according to its high degree of sensitivity to habitat disturbance."


References

The '''Tacarcuna chlorospingus''' ('''''Chlorospingus tacarcunae''''') or '''Tacarcuna bush tanager''' is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Passerellidae]], the New World sparrows. It is found in [[Colombia]] and [[Panama]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/sparrows// |title=New World sparrows, bush tanagers|website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=23 March 2026 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Tacarcuna chlorospingus has a complicated taxonomic history. It was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1924 with its current [[Binomial nomenclature|binomial]] ''Chlorospingus tacarcunae''.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Griscom |first=Ludlow |date=1924 |title=Discriptions of new birds from Panama and Costa Rica |url=https://digitallibrary.amnh.org/items/b245ac16-343a-49b1-a0d6-cdf3e08f64a7 |journal=American Museum Novitates |issue=141 |pages=11–12 |access-date=April 22, 2026 }}</ref> During the next two decades different authors treated it as a subspecies what are now the [[yellow-throated chlorospingus]] (''C. flavigularis'') or the [[common chlorospingus]] (''C. flavopectus''). From about 1976 it has been again treated as a full species.<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, D. F. Lane, L, N. Naka, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 27 February 2026. A classification of the bird species of South America. South American Classification Committee associated with the International Ornithologists' Union. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved February 28, 2026</ref> During this period the ''Chlorospingus'' species were members of the family [[Thraupidae]], the "true" tanagers, though their position within the family were uncertain.<ref name=AOU1998>{{cite book | last =<!--Not stated--> | first =<!--Not stated--> |  title =Check-list of North American Birds | publisher =American Ornithologists' Union |edition =7th | date =1998 | location =Washington, D.C. | pages =571 }}</ref> Based on studies published in 2002, 2003, and 2007, beginning in 2010 taxonomic systems transferred ''Chlorospingus'' to its present sparrow family.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop426.htm |title=Proposal (426) Place ''Chlorospingus'' in the Emberizidae |last=Remsen |first=J. V. |date=March 2010  |publisher=South American Classification Committee |access-date=April 20, 2026 |quote=}} The proposal was accepted in August 2010.</ref><ref name=52Supp>R. Terry Chesser, Richard C. Banks, F. Keith Barker, Carla Cicero, Jon L. Dunn, Andrew W. Kratter, Irby J. Lovette, Pamela C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, Jr., James D. Rising, Douglas F. Stotz, and Kevin Winker. "Fifty-second supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union ''Check-list of North American Birds''". ''The Auk'' 2011, vol. 128:600–613 retrieved October 22, 2025</ref> The ''Chlorospingus'' species were originally called "bush tanagers" or "bush-tanagers". Beginning in 2013 systems began changing the English name of the members of the genus to "chlorospingus" because they were no longer considered tanagers.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop579.htm |title=Proposal (579) Change the English names of ''Chlorospingus'' species from 'Bush-Tanager' to 'Chlorospingus' |last=Remsen |first=J. V. |date=May 2013  |publisher=South American Classification Committee |access-date=April 20, 2026 |quote=}} The proposal was accepted in July 2013.</ref><ref name=55Supp>R. Terry Chesser, Richard C. Banks, Carla Cicero, Jon L. Dunn, Andrew W. Kratter, Irby J. Lovette, Adolfo G. Navarro-Sigüebzam, Pamela C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, Jr., James D. Rising, Douglas F. Stotz, and Kevin Winker. "Fifty-fifth supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union ''Check-list of North American Birds''". ''The Auk'' 2014, vol. 131(4) retrieved April 8, 2023</ref> 

The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC15.1/>

==Description==

The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is {{convert|13|to|14|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs about {{convert|18|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a mostly dark olive head with a pale yellow throat. Their upperparts are dark olive. Their flight feathers are dusky with greenish yellow edges on the [[Primaries (birds)|primaries]], olive edges on the [[Secondaries (birds)|secondaries]], and dark olive green edges on the [[tertials]]. Their tail is dusky with an olive tinge and olive green feather edges. Their upper breast is dull yellow, their sides, flanks, and undertail coverts greenish, and the middle of their breast and their belly dull yellowish. Both sexes have a pale orange iris, a black bill, and brownish gray legs and feet.<ref name=TACH-BOW>Avendaño, J. E. (2020). Tacarcuna Chlorospingus (''Chlorospingus tacarcunae''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.tabtan1.01 retrieved April 22, 2026</ref><ref name=McMullan>{{cite book | last1 =McMullan | first1 =Miles | last2 =Donegan | first2 =Thomas M. | last3 =Quevedo | first3 =Alonso |  title = Field Guide to the Birds of Colombia | publisher = Fundación ProAves| date =2010 | location =Bogotá | pages =209  |isbn =978-0-9827615-0-2 }}</ref> 

==Distribution and habitat==

The Tacarcuna chlorospingus has a highly [[disjunct distribution]]. It is found on individual or abutting mountains from eastern [[Panamá Province|Panamá]] and [[Colón Province|Colón]] provinces in central Panama to [[Darién Province]] and on both sides of [[Cerro Tacarcuna]] that straddles the Panama-Colombia border.<ref name=TACH-BOW/> It inhabits the interior and edges of montane [[evergreen forest]] and also [[elfin forest]] in the subtropical and lower temperate zones.<ref name=AOU1998/><ref name=TACH-BOW/> In elevation it ranges overall between {{convert|850|and|1500|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=AOU1998/> In Panamá Province it ranges between {{convert|850|and|1000|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}} and on the Panamanian side of Cerro Tacarcuna between {{convert|1230|and|1440|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=TACH-BOW/> On the Colombian side it ranges between {{convert|1200|and|1500|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=McMullan/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is a year-round resident.<ref name=TACH-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is known to feed on insects and is assumed to feed on fruits as well like others of its genus. It forages at all levels of the forest. It forages in pairs and groups of up to about six individuals and regularly joins [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s.<ref name=TACH-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The Tacarcuna chlorospingus is thought to breed between February and August. The one known nest was in a [[bromeliad]] clump on a branch about {{convert|6.5|m|ft|round=5|abbr=on}} above the ground. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.<ref name=TACH-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Chlorospingus-tacarcunae |species=the Tacarcuna chlorospingus}}
===Vocalization===

As of April 2026 [[xeno-canto]] had three recordings of Tacarcuna chlorospingus calls; the Cornell Lab's [[Macaulay Library]] had 15 others. Neither archive had recordings of its song.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Chlorospingus-tacarcunae |title=Tacarcuna Chlorospingus ''Chlorospingus tacarcunae'' |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=2026 |website=xeno-canto |access-date=January 22, 2026 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://search.macaulaylibrary.org/catalog?taxonCode=tabtan1&mediaType=audio&sort=rating_rank_desc |title=Tacarcuna Chlorospingus ''Chlorospingus tacarcunae'' |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=2026 |website=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=April 22, 2026}}</ref> The species' call is described as "a single high-pitched ''tseeeu!''".<ref name=TACH-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Tacarcuna chlorospingus as being of Least Concern. It has a restricted range and its estimated population of at least 20,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. The only expected threat is stress on its habitat due to climate change.<ref name=IUCN/> "[The] Tacarcuna Chlorospingus has been always recorded in pristine or well conserved habitats according to its high degree of sensitivity to habitat disturbance."<ref name=TACH-BOW/>
